summary:
Toyotomi Hideyoshi (1537–1598 AD) completed the reunification of Japan begun by [[event:3236|Oda Nobunaga]], rising from the son of a peasant foot-soldier to become the supreme ruler of Japan — one of history's most remarkable rags-to-riches political careers. After Nobunaga's assassination in 1582, Hideyoshi moved with extraordinary speed to avenge his lord, defeat Akechi Mitsuhide at the Battle of Yamazaki (within days of the assassination), and outmaneuver Nobunaga's surviving generals to claim succession. By 1590, through a combination of military campaigns and skilled diplomacy, he had unified all of Japan — establishing a social hierarchy that formalized the distinction between samurai, peasant, and artisan, and conducting a nationwide cadastral survey (taiko-kenchi) that measured agricultural land productivity and formed the basis for a new tax system.

Hideyoshi's ambitions extended beyond Japan: his two invasions of Korea (1592–1593 and 1597–1598), the [[event:2420|Imjin War]], aimed at conquering Korea and ultimately Ming China — an imperial fantasy that consumed enormous Japanese resources and ended in disaster. The Korean admiral Yi Sun-sin's ironclad warships (geobukseon, 'turtle ships') devastated Japanese supply lines, Ming Chinese intervention reinforced Korean resistance, and Hideyoshi died in 1598 with the invasions still ongoing. His domestic policies — the sword hunt (1588) that disarmed the peasantry and fixed the samurai class as a hereditary military elite, and the expulsion order for Christian missionaries (1587, inconsistently enforced during his lifetime) — shaped Japanese society for centuries. His designated successor coalition, the Council of Five Regents, collapsed into conflict within two years of his death, leading to [[event:3308|Tokugawa Ieyasu's]] final victory at [[event:2421|Sekigahara]].
